title: Your first HTTP server
instruction:
Three lines of Go start a web server:

```
http.HandleFunc("/", handler)
http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)
```

`HandleFunc` registers a route. `ListenAndServe` starts listening on port 8080.

The handler function takes two parameters:
- `w http.ResponseWriter` — write your response here
- `r *http.Request` — the incoming request

Run this. It simulates a single request to show the output without blocking.
successMessage: An HTTP handler in Go: two parameters, write to w, done. In a real server you would call http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) and your handler would serve real requests.

title: Writing a response
instruction:
`http.ResponseWriter` is an `io.Writer` — you can use `fmt.Fprintln`, `fmt.Fprintf`, or `w.Write()` to send a response.

You can also set the status code and headers before writing:
```
w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)       // 200
w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "text/plain")
```

Add a second handler `greetHandler` that sets the `Content-Type` to `text/plain` and responds with `"Hello, [name]!"` where name comes from the URL query parameter `name`. Then call it with name `"Alex"`.

codeChecks.message: Get the name parameter: r.URL.Query().Get("name")
Write the response to w using fmt.Fprintf or fmt.Fprintln.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Query parameters are read with r.URL.Query().Get("key"). Headers are set before writing the body. This is the foundation of every Go HTTP handler.

title: Status codes
instruction:
HTTP status codes tell the client what happened:
- `200` — OK
- `201` — Created
- `400` — Bad Request
- `404` — Not Found
- `500` — Internal Server Error

`w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)` — use the named constants, not magic numbers.

Run this. Two requests — one found, one not. Add the missing `404` case: if `id` is not `"42"`, write a `404` status and `"user not found"` message.

codeChecks.message: Write the 404 status: w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)
Write "user not found" in the body.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 200 for found, 404 for not found. Always use http.StatusXxx constants — they are self-documenting and less error-prone than raw numbers.

title: Responding with JSON
instruction:
Most APIs respond with JSON. The pattern:
1. Set `Content-Type: application/json`
2. Use `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(value)` to write JSON directly to the response

Add a second user to the `users` map (any name for id `"7"`), then handle it in `userHandler` and return JSON.

codeChecks.message: Add a second user with key "7" to the users map.
Replace the TODO comment with your user entry.

successMessage: JSON responses, status codes, 404 handling — this is a real API pattern. json.NewEncoder(w).Encode writes directly to the response without allocating an extra buffer.

title: Reading a request body
instruction:
For POST and PUT requests, the client sends data in the request body.

Decode it with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v)`.

Always close the body: `defer r.Body.Close()`

Run this. The handler reads a JSON body and echoes back the user's name. Change the request body to your own name and email.
hint: Change the body JSON from Alex to your own name.

codeChecks.message: Change the name in the body JSON to your own name.

successMessage: POST body decoded in two lines. json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req) handles everything — parsing, type conversion, error reporting. The 201 Created status signals a successful creation.

title: Multiple routes
instruction:
Register multiple routes with `http.HandleFunc`:

```
http.HandleFunc("/", homeHandler)
http.HandleFunc("/users", usersHandler)
http.HandleFunc("/health", healthHandler)
```

Add a third route `/about` that responds with `"About this API"`.
hint: http.HandleFunc("/about", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { fmt.Fprintln(w, "About this API") })

codeChecks.message: Register an /about route: mux.HandleFunc("/about", ...)
The /about handler should respond with "About this API".
Replace the TODO comment with your route.

successMessage: Three routes, three handlers. http.NewServeMux() is Go's built-in router. For simple APIs it is all you need — no third-party router required.

title: Method checking — GET vs POST
instruction:
REST APIs use different HTTP methods for different operations:
- `GET` — read data
- `POST` — create data
- `PUT` / `PATCH` — update data
- `DELETE` — delete data

Check the method with `r.Method`.

Add a `case http.MethodPost` that responds with `"Creating a user..."` and status 201.
hint: case http.MethodPost:  w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated)  fmt.Fprintln(w, "Creating a user...")

codeChecks.message: Add case http.MethodPost: to the switch.
Use w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated) for 201.
Respond with "Creating a user..." for POST.
Replace the TODO comment with your POST case.

successMessage: GET lists, POST creates, DELETE gets 405. Method checking keeps your API semantically correct — different actions on the same URL, different HTTP methods.

title: Middleware
hint: func timingMiddleware(next http.HandlerFunc) http.HandlerFunc { return func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { next(w, r); fmt.Println("Handler finished") } }

codeChecks.message: Define a timingMiddleware function.
Print "Handler finished" after calling next.
Wrap the handler with timingMiddleware.
Replace the TODO comments with your middleware.

successMessage: The middleware chain: logging → timing → handler → timing finishes. Middlewares are composable — stack as many as you need. Every real Go server uses this pattern.

title: Making an HTTP GET request
instruction:
Go is not just a server — it is also a great HTTP client.

`http.Get(url)` makes a GET request and returns a response.

Always close the body: `defer resp.Body.Close()`

Read the body with `io.ReadAll(resp.Body)`.

Run this — it calls a public JSON API and prints the status and first 100 characters of the response.
successMessage: http.Get, check the error, defer close, read the body. Three steps for every HTTP client call. The same pattern works for any URL — internal services, third-party APIs, webhooks.

title: Making an HTTP POST request
instruction:
`http.Post` sends a POST with a body:

```
resp, err := http.Post(url, "application/json", strings.NewReader(body))
```

For more control (custom headers, timeouts), use `http.NewRequest` + `http.Client`.

Run this. It POSTs JSON to httpbin which echoes back what you sent. Change the payload name to your own.
hint: Change "Alex" in the JSON payload to your name.
starter:
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"io"
	"net/http"
	"strings"
)

func main() {
	payload := `{"name":"Alex","course":"Go"}`
	resp, err := http.Post(
		"https://httpbin.org/post",
		"application/json",
		strings.NewReader(payload),
	)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("Error:", err)
		return
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()

	body, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
	fmt.Println("Status:", resp.StatusCode)
	_ = body
	fmt.Println("POST sent successfully")
}

codeChecks.message: Change the name in the payload to your own name.

successMessage: POST with a JSON body — the foundation of every API call you will make. http.Post for simple cases, http.NewRequest + http.Client when you need headers, timeouts, or retries.

title: Setting request headers
instruction:
Many APIs require headers — authentication tokens, content types, API keys.

Use `http.NewRequest` to build a request with custom headers before sending it:

```
req, _ := http.NewRequest("GET", url, nil)
req.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+token)
client := &http.Client{}
resp, err := client.Do(req)
```

Add a second header `X-Request-ID` with value `"abc-123"` to the request.

codeChecks.message: Set the X-Request-ID header: req.Header.Set("X-Request-ID", "abc-123")
Replace the TODO comment with your header.

successMessage: Headers set, headers read. Bearer tokens, API keys, request IDs, CORS headers — all follow the same pattern. r.Header.Get on the server, req.Header.Set on the client.

title: Build a mini REST API
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a mini REST API for a todo list:

1. Define a `Todo` struct with `ID` int, `Title` string, `Done` bool (all JSON tagged)
2. Create a `[]Todo` slice with 2 initial todos
3. Write a `listHandler` that responds with the todos as JSON
4. Write an `addHandler` that reads a JSON body, appends a new Todo, and responds with 201
5. Route `GET /todos` → listHandler, `POST /todos` → addHandler (check `r.Method`)
6. Test: GET first, then POST a new todo, then GET again — the list should grow

Expected final GET output includes all 3 todos as JSON.
hint: Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(todos) to respond. Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&newTodo) to read the body.

codeChecks.message: Define a Todo struct.
Add JSON tags to the Todo struct fields.
Define a listHandler function.
Define an addHandler function.
Check r.Method to route GET vs POST.
Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode to respond with JSON.
Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode to read the POST body.
Return 201 Created for successful POST.

successMessage: A working REST API — list, add, check. GET returns JSON, POST reads JSON and appends, the list grows. This is how every Go API is structured in production. You just built one.
